does: This paper tests the hypothesis that knowledge-intensive scientific decision-making by AI agents is limited not by model quality or reasoning scaffolds, but by access to authoritative evidence. The authors conduct a controlled three-arm ablation study on a drug-asset valuation agent: a baseline web-only LLM analyst; a version augmented with public structured tools, valuation frameworks, verification mechanisms and governance policies; and a version additionally equipped with proprietary Noah AI corpus containing curated pipeline, clinical trial, and deal intelligence. Across a stratified 13-asset benchmark, they measure performance through calibration metrics, audit discipline, and recovery of curated gold-standard competitive records. Results show that enhanced reasoning and governance improve calibration and objectivity metrics modestly, but only access to proprietary curated evidence enables near-complete recovery of ground truth valuations, particularly on long-tail assets where public information proves insufficient.
connects: This work extends recent research on AI agents for knowledge-intensive tasks, building on critiques of reasoning-first approaches that prioritize chain-of-thought, prompting, and verification scaffolds. It responds to the interpretability and evaluation literature questioning whether model capability gains translate to real-world performance without domain-appropriate data foundations. The paper combines ideas from structured decision-making frameworks, valuation methodology playbooks, and quality control through red-teaming and objectivity policies developed in applied ML systems. It relates to broader discussions in AI evaluation about benchmark design, gold standards, and the role of curated datasets in benchmarking agents, while also engaging with institutional knowledge management and the quantification of proprietary information value in competitive intelligence.
enables: This work establishes evidence substrate quality as a measurable bottleneck for AI scientist agents and points toward future research on knowledge-base architecture for specialized domains. It enables design of agent systems prioritizing curation and access control alongside inference—suggesting that institutional investment in evidence quality may yield higher returns than scaling models. The stratified benchmark methodology and recovery metrics provide a framework for assessing agent performance across data availability regimes, which could be adapted to other knowledge-intensive domains like law, medicine, or materials science. The findings suggest follow-up work on optimal information architecture for AI agents, the economics of proprietary data in agent systems, and methods for graceful degradation when authoritative evidence is unavailable, potentially including hybrid human-AI workflows that identify when agent decisions exceed evidence quality.
